topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun slanted through the dusty window, illuminating motes dancing in the air, and I couldn't settle. My fingers kept tracing the grain of the wooden desk, a repetitive, anxious motion. I was sorting through old school papers, a task I’d been putting off for months, when I found it: a faded photograph tucked inside a textbook. It was a picture of a young boy, grinning widely, standing beside a woman with kind eyes. My breath hitched. The face in the photo was undeniably my son. And the woman… the woman was Ms. Eleanor Vance. My son’s teacher. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, pulling at my chest. I reread the caption, my eyes blurring. It was a coincidence, surely. But the air in the room suddenly felt too thin, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I needed to move, to do anything other than stare at the glossy paper.

[story 2]

Every minute stretched into an eternity in the quiet house. I paced from the kitchen to the living room, the worn floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. My jaw was tight, my hands clenched into fists at my sides. I was supposed to be relaxing, enjoying the evening, but my mind was a frantic carousel, spinning through impossible scenarios. Then, while cleaning out a box of forgotten school supplies, I unearthed a syllabus. The names were printed in neat, familiar script. My heart hammered against my ribs as I scanned the roster. There, listed under 'Third Grade,' was my son’s name, and next to it, the title: Ms. Eleanor Vance. A wave of disorientation washed over me, followed by a dizzying sense of wrongness. I stopped breathing for a moment, just letting the sheer weight of the realization settle. Why did this feel so profoundly unsettling?

[story 3]

I couldn't focus on anything. The ticking of the grandfather clock in the hall seemed deafening, each second an unbearable pressure. I kept checking my phone, a nervous habit, though I knew it wouldn't change anything. I felt an overwhelming urge to rearrange everything in the room—to shove books into different stacks, to change the orientation of the furniture—anything to disrupt the stagnant atmosphere. While rummaging through a storage bin in the attic, I found a collection of old yearbooks. My gaze snagged on one, the pages brittle with age. There, smiling brightly in the corner, was my child. And beside him, the familiar, gentle face of Ms. Vance. A strange, hollow sensation spread through my limbs. My carefully constructed sense of normalcy had just fractured, leaving me standing utterly still, the world tilting slightly on its axis.
